question_text: Two countries have the same population and electricity demand. Country A generates 80% from coal and 20% from renewables (carbon footprint: 600 kg CO₂ per person per year). Country B generates 30% from coal, 50% from nuclear, and 20% from renewables (carbon footprint: 150 kg CO₂ per person per year). If both countries want to reduce emissions by 50% over 10 years, which faces the greater challenge and why?
correct_answer: Country A faces a greater challenge because it must reduce from 600 to 300 kg CO₂ per person (reducing coal dependence from 80%), requiring replacement of massive coal infrastructure. Country B only needs to reduce from 150 to 75 kg CO₂ per person and already has diverse sources. Replacing coal infrastructure is expensive and slow.
explanation: This requires comparing absolute reductions needed and understanding that shifting away from coal (the most carbon-intensive source) requires replacing significant infrastructure. Country A's heavy coal dependence makes the transition more difficult and costly than Country B's already-diversified mix.
